Let me make sure I understand you correctly.
I might be reading this wrong, so please correct me.
Before we dive in, how are things on your side?
How does your team usually prefer to decide this?
Let us find an approach that works for everyone here.
So, how did the call with Frankfurt and Tokyo go? I saw a few long silences on my screen.

Better than I expected. But you are right about the silence. When the Tokyo team went quiet, I almost misread it. I thought, they might be unhappy. Then I remembered: in their team, a pause usually means they are still building agreement.
Exactly. A pause there is not a no. And Frankfurt? They can be very direct.
Very. Klaus said the timeline simply does not work. At first that came across as harsh, but it was not personal. For them, directness is just the norm; it is actually a sign of respect.
It must be hard to balance both styles in one call.
It was. So I adapted. With Frankfurt I went straight to the numbers; with Tokyo I slowed down and left room for them to think. And I made sure nobody had to lose face when we disagreed.
That is the skill. And a little small talk never hurts; here in Buenos Aires the rapport has to come first, or nothing moves.
I am learning that. Read the room, adapt the style, protect the relationship. The etiquette changes, but respect is the one thing that travels everywhere.
She read the room, saw the silence, and thought it might mean they were still deciding.
Too much directness can come across as blunt, so I adapt and add a little warmth.
They have not replied yet; they might be waiting for consensus.
Punctuality must be very important to them.
That cannot mean he disagrees; in his team, silence means he is still thinking.
Let me build some rapport first; a few minutes of small talk before business.

Okay, before this call, let me read the situation. I have three offices on the line: our partners in Tokyo, our supplier in Frankfurt, and Elena's team in Buenos Aires. They do not all communicate the same way, so I cannot use one single style. The Frankfurt team values directness, so they might find a long warm-up strange; with them, I should get to the point quickly. The Tokyo office is more careful, so a quiet pause from them does not mean a no. It might mean they are still building consensus, and I must not misread that silence. In Buenos Aires, the relationship comes first, so a few minutes of small talk will build rapport before we touch the numbers. My job today is not to be the loudest voice. It is to read the room, adapt my tone to each side, and make sure nobody has to lose face. If I get the etiquette right, the content will land. So let me listen more than I speak.

Hi Victor, it is Nadia. Before your cross-cultural call, three quick reminders. First, treat every cultural pattern as a tendency, not a rule. People are individuals, so read the person in front of you, not the stereotype. A norm tells you what might be happening; it never tells you for certain. Second, when a signal confuses you, do not guess in silence. Check it. Say, I want to make sure I understand you correctly, and let them clarify. That one habit prevents most misunderstandings before they grow. Third, protect the relationship over the point. If someone is wrong, give them a way to save face, and never correct a senior colleague in front of their team. And watch your own directness. What feels honest to you can come across as blunt to someone else, so soften the edges and add a little warmth. Read the room, adapt, and stay curious. You are good at this, Victor. Go build the bridge.
